policy_statement: Economically valorise biomass by installing intermunicipal systems for collecting and interim storage of biomass residues from forestry and agricultural activities in rural areas.
mechanism_description: Creating systems to collect and store forestry and agricultural residues enables their productive use (e.g., energy/materials), reducing emissions from unmanaged residue decay/burning and displacing fossil fuels.
primary_causal_pathway: Residue management system → ↓ open burning/decay emissions + Biomass utilization → Fossil fuel displacement → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Establish organized collection, aggregation, interim storage, and logistics for forestry/agricultural residues that would otherwise be left to decay or be burned on-site, enabling their use in bioenergy (heat/power/biogas) or bio-based materials.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Avoids direct combustion emissions from open burning and reduces methane/CO₂ emissions from unmanaged decomposition; when residues are used for energy/materials, they substitute for fossil fuels and emissions-intensive products (with net benefit depending on lifecycle and combustion controls).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net greenhouse gas emissions (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O) relative to baseline residue handling and fossil energy/material supply.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions by preventing uncontrolled residue emissions (burning/decay) and displacing fossil fuel use with collected biomass.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Residue collection/utilization changes land-use/biomass management, which directly avoids unmanaged residue emissions and enables substitution for fossil fuels, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although improved residue management can aid adaptation (e.g., reduced fuel loads and wildfire severity) and resource efficiency (use of “waste” biomass), the most direct and largest climate-relevant effect common to this mechanism is emissions reduction (mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: If biomass use is inefficient, poorly controlled (high black carbon), or causes indirect land-use change, mitigation benefits may be reduced; however, those are implementation- and context-specific and do not change the canonical dominant intent/pathway of the mechanism.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that builds systems to collect/store residues for productive use inherently targets avoided unmanaged emissions and/or fossil displacement as the primary climate pathway, so “Mitigation” consistently fits across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy should be tagged additionally as wildfire-risk adaptation, air-quality improvement, or circular-economy/resource-efficiency is policy- and evidence-dependent and should be assigned at the individual policy level, not at this mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include (a) policies framed mainly for wildfire risk reduction (adaptation co-primary in some contexts), (b) projects where residues are transported long distances or converted inefficiently, eroding net mitigation, and (c) biomass combustion with high short-lived climate pollutant emissions; these affect realized benefits but not the dominant intended causal pathway of this mechanism.
key_indicators: “installation of Intermunicipal Systems”; “collection and interim storage”; “biomass left over from forestry and agricultural activities”; “economic valorisation of biomass”
co_benefits: Potential wildfire risk reduction and improved rural waste/residue management (not explicitly stated as objectives).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be construed as a planning/coordination measure (inter-municipal system), but the operative action is the installation of collection/storage infrastructure, so infrastructure-investment is the best fit.
